questions.question:
Select the word that fits the analogy:

Build : Building :: Grow : ________

A. Grown  B. Grew  C. Growth  D. Growed
questions.to_find: The word that completes the analogy Grow : ________ in the same way that Build relates to Building

understand.plain: The analogy pairs a verb with its noun form that represents the result or product of the action. 'Build' gives 'Building' — a noun meaning the act or product of building. We need the equivalent noun for 'Grow'. 'Growth' is the noun derived from 'grow' that captures the same concept.

keywords.explain: A comparison showing that two pairs share the same relationship
keywords.example: Hot : Heat :: Cold : Coldness — adjective to noun

keywords.explain: 'Building' here functions as a gerund/noun (the act or result of building), not a present participle
keywords.example: Swimming is healthy — 'swimming' is a gerund (noun form of 'swim')

keywords.explain: Adding suffixes to verbs to form nouns: build -> building, grow -> growth
keywords.example: grow -> growth, know -> knowledge, strong -> strength

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this word analogy question.

given.aim: Find the word that completes the analogy Build:Building::Grow:______

terms.meaning: Verb 'build' paired with its noun form 'building' (the act or structure produced by building)
terms.connects: Sets the pattern: verb -> noun derived from that verb

terms.meaning: A verb meaning to increase in size, develop, or advance
terms.connects: The second verb whose noun form we must identify

terms.meaning: The noun form of 'grow', meaning the process or result of growing
terms.connects: Mirrors the relationship Build->Building by being the noun derived from 'grow'

given.plan: Identify the relationship in the given pair (verb -> derived noun), then apply it to 'Grow' by finding its corresponding noun. 'Growth' is the standard noun derived from 'grow'.

steps.title: Identify the relationship in the given pair
steps.formula_raw: Verb -> Noun (the product or process of the verb)
steps.apply: Build (verb) -> Building (noun meaning the act/result of building). The relationship is: verb transforms into a derived noun.
steps.note: Note that 'Building' here is a noun, not the -ing present participle.

steps.title: Apply the relationship to 'Grow'
steps.apply: Grown (past participle, not a noun), Grew (past tense), Growed (incorrect form), Growth (noun, the process/result of growing). The noun derived from 'grow' is 'Growth'.
steps.note: Only 'Growth' functions as a noun in the same way that 'Building' is a noun, making it the correct answer.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal analogy question requiring knowledge of word derivation; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Select the word that fits the analogy:

White : Whitening :: Light : _________

A. Lightning  B. Lightening  C. Lighting  D. Enlightening
questions.to_find: The word that completes the analogy Light : ________ in the same way that White relates to Whitening

understand.plain: The analogy relates an adjective to its -ening form that means the process of becoming or making something more of that quality. 'White' -> 'Whitening' means making something white or becoming white. 'Light' -> 'Lightening' means making something lighter (brighter or less heavy). This is a derivational morphology pattern: adjective + -ening.

keywords.explain: The process of making something white or becoming white; formed by adding -ening to the adjective 'white'
keywords.example: Teeth whitening is a common cosmetic procedure.

keywords.explain: The process of making something lighter in colour or weight; formed from the adjective 'light' + -ening
keywords.example: The lightening of her hair took three sessions.

keywords.explain: 'Lightning' (no e) is the electrical discharge in a storm; 'Lightening' (with e) is the verb/noun meaning making lighter
keywords.example: Lightning struck the tree. / The lightening of the colour was gradual.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this word analogy question.

given.aim: Find the word that completes the analogy White:Whitening::Light:________

terms.meaning: Adjective 'white' paired with 'whitening' — the process of becoming or making white
terms.connects: Establishes pattern: adjective + ening -> process of that quality

terms.meaning: An adjective meaning bright or not heavy; the base word for the analogy
terms.example: A light colour reflects more sunlight.
terms.connects: The second adjective whose -ening form we must identify

terms.meaning: The process of making something lighter (in colour or weight); formed as light + ening
terms.example: The lightening of sky at dawn signals sunrise.
terms.connects: Mirrors White->Whitening by applying the same suffix rule to 'light'

given.plan: Identify the morphological pattern (adjective + -ening), then apply it to 'Light'. Eliminate distractors: Lightning (storm discharge), Lighting (illumination setup), Enlightening (educating/informing).

steps.title: Identify the morphological pattern
steps.formula_raw: Adjective + ening -> process of becoming/making that quality
steps.apply: White + ening = Whitening (making/becoming white). So the pattern is: base adjective + ening.
steps.note: The suffix '-ening' is added to the adjective form to express a process of change.

steps.title: Apply pattern to 'Light' and eliminate distractors
steps.apply: A. Lightning — no 'e', refers to electrical storm discharge (unrelated). B. Lightening — light + ening, process of making lighter. C. Lighting — different morphological form; means arrangement of lights. D. Enlightening — prefix 'en' added, changes meaning to 'making wiser'.
steps.note: Only 'Lightening' exactly parallels the White->Whitening pattern.

questions.formula_note: This is a verbal analogy question about morphological derivation; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
Planting : Seed :: Raising : _________
(By word meaning)

A. Child  B. Temperature  C. Height  D. Lift
questions.to_find: The word that completes the analogy Raising : ________ by the same semantic relationship as Planting : Seed

understand.plain: The question asks us to find the semantic relationship between 'Planting' and 'Seed', then apply it to 'Raising'. Planting is the action and Seed is what you plant — the direct object of the verb. Similarly, Raising means to bring up or nurture, and a 'Child' is what you raise. The hint '(By word meaning)' emphasizes that we must think about the direct object of the action verb.

keywords.explain: The meaning-based connection between words, here: action -> direct object (what is acted upon)
keywords.example: Cooking : Food — you cook food; Writing : Letter — you write a letter

keywords.explain: The act of putting seeds in soil so they can grow; the direct object is the seed
keywords.example: Planting a seed in fertile soil is the first step of farming.

keywords.explain: In the context of nurturing, to raise means to bring up a child; the direct object is a child
keywords.example: Raising a child requires patience, care, and guidance.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this semantic analogy question.

given.aim: Find what 'Raising' acts upon, mirroring how 'Planting' acts upon 'Seed'

terms.meaning: You plant a seed — seed is the direct object of planting
terms.example: Planting seeds in spring leads to harvest in autumn.
terms.connects: Establishes the pattern: gerund (action) -> what is directly acted upon

terms.meaning: To nurture and bring up; in word meaning, to raise a child is the primary collocation
terms.example: Raising children is one of life's most rewarding challenges.
terms.connects: The action whose direct object must be identified

terms.meaning: The natural direct object of 'raising' in the sense of nurturing and bringing up
terms.example: She devoted twenty years to raising her child.
terms.connects: Mirrors 'Seed' as the thing that is directly 'raised' just as a seed is 'planted'

given.plan: Find what you directly 'raise' by meaning. Eliminate: Temperature (you raise it but that is a measurement, not a natural semantic pair like planting/seed), Height (abstract property), Lift (synonym, not an object).

steps.title: Analyse the given pair
steps.formula_raw: Action verb -> direct object of that action
steps.apply: Planting -> Seed. You plant a seed. Relationship: verb refers to nurturing/cultivating something, and the paired word is the natural object being nurtured.
steps.note: The hint 'By word meaning' tells us to focus on the common semantic collocate of the verb.

steps.title: Apply pattern to 'Raising'
steps.apply: A. Child — you raise a child (nurture from infancy to adulthood). B. Temperature — raise temperature is possible but temperature is not a natural nurture object. C. Height — an abstract measurement, not something raised in the same sense. D. Lift — a synonym of raise, not an object.
steps.note: Just as a seed is the living thing you plant and nurture, a child is the living being you raise and nurture.

questions.formula_note: This is a semantic analogy question; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
The question below consists of a pair of related words followed by four pairs of words. Select the pair that best expresses the relation in the original pair.

Preamble : Constitution

A. amendment : law
B. prologue : play
C. episode : serial
D. plot : story
questions.to_find: The pair that best expresses the same relationship as Preamble : Constitution

understand.plain: A Preamble is the introductory statement that comes at the beginning of a Constitution, outlining its purpose and guiding principles. We need to find a pair where the first word is an introductory/prefatory section that comes at the start of the second word (a larger work). A Prologue is the introductory section of a Play, making it the perfect parallel.

keywords.explain: An introductory statement placed at the very beginning of a formal document (like a Constitution) that explains its purpose
keywords.example: The Preamble to the Indian Constitution begins with 'We, the people of India...'

keywords.explain: An introductory section or speech at the beginning of a literary work or play, setting the stage for what follows
keywords.example: The prologue to Romeo and Juliet tells the audience the play's ending in advance.

keywords.explain: The relationship where the first item is the opening/prefatory component of the second item
keywords.example: Introduction : Book, Preface : Novel, Prologue : Play

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this word-pair analogy question.

given.aim: Identify the relationship in Preamble:Constitution and find the matching pair

terms.meaning: The Preamble is the introductory/prefatory opening section of a Constitution
terms.example: The Preamble of the US Constitution begins 'We the People...'
terms.connects: Establishes the pattern: introductory section -> the larger formal document it introduces

terms.meaning: A Prologue is the introductory section delivered at the beginning of a Play
terms.example: Shakespeare used prologues to set the scene before the main action began.
terms.connects: Exactly mirrors Preamble:Constitution — introductory section -> the larger work it opens

terms.meaning: Amendment:Law (addition, not opening section), Episode:Serial (a part, not the intro), Plot:Story (element within, not an opening)
terms.example: An amendment changes existing law; it does not introduce it.
terms.connects: None of the distractors share the specific 'introductory opening section' relationship

given.plan: Find which pair has an 'introductory opening section -> larger work' relationship. Prologue:Play is the only pair where the first term is specifically the opening introductory section of the second.

steps.title: Determine the precise relationship
steps.formula_raw: First word = introductory opening section of Second word
steps.apply: Preamble is the formal introductory statement placed at the start of a Constitution. It is not just any part — it is specifically the opening prefatory statement.
steps.note: The key precision: Preamble is specifically an OPENING / INTRODUCTORY part, not just any part.

steps.title: Test each option
steps.formula_raw: Is the first word the introductory opening of the second?
steps.apply: A. Amendment:Law — An amendment is an addition/change to existing law, not its opening section. B. Prologue:Play — A prologue is the introductory section at the start of a play. Matches. C. Episode:Serial — An episode is a unit of a serial, not the introduction. D. Plot:Story — Plot is a narrative element within a story, not an introductory section.
steps.note: Only B (Prologue:Play) has the same introductory-opening relationship as Preamble:Constitution.

questions.formula_note: This is a word-pair analogy question requiring understanding of document structure; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
The question below consists of a pair of related words followed by four pairs of words. Select the pair that best expresses the relation in the original pair.

Erudition : Scholar

A. steadfast : mercurial
B. competence : strict
C. skill : craftsman
D. nurse : doctor
questions.to_find: The pair that best expresses the same relationship as Erudition : Scholar

understand.plain: Erudition means great depth of knowledge or learning, and it is the defining characteristic of a Scholar. The relationship is: the first word is the hallmark quality or defining trait of the person described by the second word. A 'Skill' is the defining characteristic of a 'Craftsman', making it the closest parallel.

keywords.explain: Deep, extensive knowledge acquired through study; the hallmark quality of a scholar
keywords.example: Her erudition in classical literature was evident from her lectures.

keywords.explain: A person who has studied a subject deeply and is known for their learning and knowledge
keywords.example: A scholar is defined by their erudition — deep, broad knowledge.

keywords.explain: The pattern where the first word is the essential, defining quality that identifies the person named by the second word
keywords.example: Courage : Warrior, Wisdom : Sage, Skill : Craftsman

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this word-pair analogy question.

given.aim: Find the pair where the first word is the defining characteristic of the person in the second word

terms.meaning: Erudition (deep knowledge) is the defining quality that makes someone a Scholar
terms.example: Without erudition, one cannot truly be called a scholar.
terms.connects: Establishes pattern: defining characteristic -> the person known for that characteristic

terms.meaning: Skill (proficiency in a trade) is the defining quality of a Craftsman
terms.example: A craftsman is defined by the skill they bring to their craft.
terms.connects: Mirrors Erudition:Scholar — the defining quality of the professional

terms.meaning: Steadfast:Mercurial (antonyms), Competence:Strict (unrelated attributes), Nurse:Doctor (different professions, not quality-person)
terms.example: Steadfast and mercurial are opposites, not a quality-bearer pair.
terms.connects: None of A, B, D represent a defining-quality-to-person relationship

given.plan: Verify each option against the pattern 'first word is the hallmark quality of the person (second word)'. Only C (skill:craftsman) fits this pattern precisely.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.formula_raw: Hallmark quality -> the professional defined by that quality
steps.apply: Erudition is the hallmark of a Scholar. Without erudition, one is not a true scholar. The relationship is: quality -> professional embodiment of that quality.
steps.note: This is stronger than 'associated with' — it is the defining, necessary characteristic.

steps.title: Test each option
steps.formula_raw: Is first word the defining quality of the person described by the second word?
steps.apply: A. Steadfast:Mercurial — these are antonym adjectives, not quality-person. B. Competence:Strict — 'strict' is a behavioral style, not a profession defined by 'competence'. C. Skill:Craftsman — skill is the defining hallmark of a craftsman. Matches perfectly. D. Nurse:Doctor — both are professions, not a quality-professional pair.
steps.note: Skill is to a craftsman what erudition is to a scholar — the essential defining attribute.

questions.formula_note: This is a word-pair analogy question about defining professional traits; no mathematical formulas apply.

questions.question:
The question below consists of a pair of related words followed by four pairs of words. Select the pair that best expresses the relation in the original pair.

Gladiator : Arena

A. dancer : stage
B. commuter : train
C. teacher : classroom
D. lawyer : courtroom
questions.to_find: The pair that best expresses the same relationship as Gladiator : Arena

understand.plain: A Gladiator performs or works exclusively in an Arena — the arena is the specific, dedicated venue where a gladiator practices their profession. Similarly, a Lawyer works professionally and formally in a Courtroom. The key distinction is that the arena is not just a place the gladiator visits; it is the specific professional venue defined by that profession. A courtroom is the exclusive, formal professional venue of a lawyer.

keywords.explain: A professional fighter in ancient Rome who fought in an arena as their occupation
keywords.example: Gladiators trained specifically to compete in the Colosseum arena.

keywords.explain: The specific, formal venue where gladiators fight — defined by and exclusive to that profession
keywords.example: The arena was the gladiator's exclusive professional fighting ground.

keywords.explain: The arena/venue is the defining, formal professional space associated with the practitioner
keywords.example: Surgeon : Operating Theatre, Pilot : Cockpit, Lawyer : Courtroom

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this word-pair analogy question.

given.aim: Find the pair where the second word is the specific formal professional venue of the first

terms.meaning: A gladiator's exclusive, defining professional venue is the arena
terms.example: A gladiator without an arena cannot practice their profession.
terms.connects: Establishes pattern: professional -> their dedicated formal professional venue

terms.meaning: A lawyer's formal professional venue is the courtroom — where legal proceedings take place
terms.example: A lawyer argues cases in a courtroom, which is their formal professional arena.
terms.connects: Exactly mirrors Gladiator:Arena — the courtroom is the lawyer's arena

terms.meaning: Dancer:Stage (stage is not exclusive to dancers), Commuter:Train (commuters are passengers, not professionals), Teacher:Classroom (closer but a classroom is shared with students)
terms.example: A stage is used by many types of performers including actors and musicians, not just dancers.
terms.connects: None as precisely match the exclusive formal professional venue relationship as Lawyer:Courtroom

given.plan: The arena is the gladiator's formal, exclusive professional combat venue — defined by that profession. Test each: a lawyer's formal venue (courtroom) is the closest parallel, as a courtroom is defined by and exclusive to legal proceedings.

steps.title: Identify the precise relationship
steps.formula_raw: Professional -> exclusive formal venue defined by that profession
steps.apply: Gladiator fights in an Arena — the arena is the defined, exclusive venue for gladiatorial combat. It is the formal professional space associated with the gladiator's role.
steps.note: The key is 'exclusive formal professional venue' — not just any place the person goes.

steps.title: Test each option for best match
steps.formula_raw: Is the second word the exclusive formal professional venue of the first?
steps.apply: A. Dancer:Stage — a stage is used by many performers (actors, musicians); not exclusive to dancers. B. Commuter:Train — a commuter is a traveller, not a professional; a train is a vehicle, not a professional venue. C. Teacher:Classroom — a classroom is shared with students; less 'exclusive formal venue'. D. Lawyer:Courtroom — a courtroom is the formal, official professional venue where only lawyers (and judges) practice their craft; most precise parallel.
steps.note: A courtroom is defined by legal proceedings just as an arena is defined by gladiatorial combat — making D the best answer.

questions.formula_note: This is a word-pair analogy question about professional venues; no mathematical formulas apply.
